regeneration so of course Remember that book I talked about not I haven't written but I want to about the Facebook Advertising book called signals and so the more signals and more data Facebook has the better off that lookalike is Going to be too so basically if there's more data flowing into let's say an add-to-cart or let's say not even a direct response example Let's say it's a page view Or let's say it's a video view So the more that you have in that core the better the lookalike is going to be as well because there's more accurate data as a part of that So, you know if that carts like example isn't strong enough then you can You know and if it's the add to cart to conversion percentage is low Then maybe an add to cart lookalike will not perform as well So I'm just trying to lay this out, but in the discussion that I had with agencies Sort of leading up to this a lot of my colleagues in that in the industry These are the ones that they really mentioned and agreed upon a 1% of two-time purchasers So if you get to the phase where you have multiple purchasers a lookalike of people that purchase twice as good a 2% lookalike of purchasers It's generally a little bit bigger because it's going to give Facebook a little bit more data and a little bit more room to run Which is something we're going to talk about here in just a second But that 2% of purchasers actually has in many cases people have seen that outperform Other ones so kind of interesting 10% VIP audience so we're talking about that absolute best customer list of people that have ordered more than twice or maybe they've spent over $150 US or something. And so what you can do is you can actually take a Particular product that you're launching or a particular set of products putting it in the photo post And you can take that post and we'll talk about how to do this and launch that post for page post engagement to the people That previously purchased from you and what ends up happening is what you can see on the right My six best friends and I have these and I love them We had this picture with done with them, too So you get validation of that product being purchased, which is fantastic This is something that I didn't do just want to say until about nine months ago a Year ago where you're actually thinking in the way of yeah that ad needs to be a landing page And that's a great thing for scaling because what happens as we push to less and less relevant people when we're scaling is We also work we need to validate the product right we need to give people a reason to click and what do we do? We click on comments So if you click and look at that you could see that here it is just ordered hope everything. It's great update They're exactly what I ordered. So this is a fantastic thing that's validating before they even get to your site

I don't want to annoy people that a ton that have purchased from me before but You then copy that post ID and hit enter and then you can launch the ad from there So that's something that I love to do scaling generally it really works on that Let's talk about copy so Every six months Gracie and I and and the clients we work with you throw out a lot of assumptions And we do a lot of testing on ad copy and this is important to mention ad copy is one of the things that you You don't think about as much as you scale, but again, you're you're getting to increasingly People that don't know you as much So what can you do and what are some themes to how you can do this to make it work for scaling? So shorter headline copy always wins right try to stick to having no more than 15 words if you can Eight out of ten times having a period at the end of a sentence works better than exclamation points Which is interesting and these are some tests that we've run over the last man I mean every six months we throw it out. It's super interesting Take 15% off our best-selling models back and stock grab it for it's gone with our logo buy one get one running now So again thinking about how you can use some of these tips for ad copy in scaling will really really help All right, and we're to talk about recommended placements for expansion. So a Lot of times those of us that are in The mobile news feed right that's where a lot of us are spending our time and if you let Facebook decide by default Where to show your ads they're going to show it mostly in the mobile news feed, which is completely fine But one of the best ways that you can control costs as you scale in horizontal scaling is to try to split placements meaning Showing the ads the same ad to the same targeting group But in different places so one on Instagram one on mobile news feed, etc There's a varying schools of thought some people really say hey, you know, I I Don't like to split out stuff Mostly want to keep it all together and that aggregates that on the ad set level and it gets to that 50 conversions much faster Which is completely legitimate and it's not something that I think is a bad idea either However, as you scale I think minimum you do want to try to split out Instagram because Instagram behaves differently It behaves from a creative standpoint differently and it created behaves from a from a serving standpoint differently actually so many times Your cost per conversion Maybe a lot different on Instagram than it would be on the mobile news feed and we'll talk about how to look at that

## [45:36 - 48:36]

It's especially helpful to me in the lower parts of the funnel for this Although recently I had an agency partner that contacted me and they're doing Really a lot of testing on the top of the funnel with only Instagram and it's been doing really really well but I think in the lower part of the funnel ensure that creative matches, right so meaning that you if you take let's say for example a Link post and you launch that on Facebook And it also is running on Instagram if you put a headline on that image on Facebook That will show on Instagram and sometimes it doesn't look as good and it looks a little weird And it doesn't look like it's native to the platform. It looks like an ad so maybe it wouldn't perform as well So splitting that out in the lower part of the funnel Can help achieve a better audience penetration and better performance One of the most common things that I'll see when I audit accounts I audit probably auditing meeting looking in doing a deep dive. I audit probably man 50 accounts a year at least And I'll see people losing money in the setting placement to default just letting saying hey, you know The Facebook decide again not a bad idea, but as you scale It's you want to try to control that a little bit more as you can right and especially like things like the audience network not a Super high quality placement. So if you ever want to look at this to me, this is interesting you go in You look under you pull up your ads You look campaign and you can or you can look at the ad set level and you go under the breakdown menu and hit placement And you can see that here in this example that if you look at the bottom screen Okay, well instant articles only have one purchase But it was for five dollars the right column has had five for 12 whereas you're paying More for them on the desktop and you're paying more for them on the mobile news feed So to me what this represents is alright, this is doing pretty interesting. This is a retention campaign that's running here To me this proves that you should probably split out Instagram because it's it's basically eight dollars cheaper than the mobile news Feed which is where the majority of your impressions are coming from so that's a type of thing that I'm talking about in terms of Splitting that out so experimentation that you can do with this try splitting that out in the lower part of funnel for Instagram first All right, I think that can be really helpful Try splitting maybe the desktop news feed out desktop news feed not a lot of inventory But for particularly really low audience like a low funnel audiences desktop can be good This is especially relevant as it relates to B2B. This is the thing that People are wanting you want them to fill out a longer form or you want them to take more of an action or maybe even enter into a chat with somebody You know using maybe a chat function on your website or something like that Using desktop news feed and splitting that out can be a little bit more expensive from cost per thousand impressions perspective but it can be effective from a conversion perspective because

So Facebook's auction Okay, we talked about this a little bit early on but it has two goals showing people ads they find relevant maximizing value to drive your business goals and There are three main components to the bidding side of this the auction the pacing system and advertiser controls what this technical jargon means generally speaking is that If you think about the way you're going to bid and change your bids over time The main components of this are the auction How does it relate to the rest of the people that you're competing against to show and add to that group of people? pacing meaning how much budget is it using over a certain period of time and then advertiser controls what am I doing in terms of actually upping or you know upping or Creating a lower amount on the bids and using manual piece of that over time So that's really what we're talking about when we talk about bidding for scale

What's the ad quality? post-click experience You know predicted versus you know sort of actual positive negative feedback That's kind of interesting in terms of that auction. So as you think about The conversion bidding options generally speaking you have one day click seven day click one day click review seven day click review okay What each of these things generally means is Are people or is your product or is your service something that people click on the ad and go through and If you did a seven day click window that they would click on it within seven days They would also go back and convert okay if your experience and an ad is more passive Then you may want to think about including a view as well meaning people are going to look at this and they're going to view it So it's all about how that user is responding to this and I realized I'm trying to simplify it as much as I can But thinking about okay, you know is something is my product a under $60 us or under $50 us or a you know lower price Product that people are going to click and go and convert or is it something that they are going to probably look at and maybe think about and go back And sometimes you don't know and this is why you have to do a lot of testing so There's three main options that you have in relation to this that I'm going to focus on the conversion bidding options There's lowest cost bidding which is automatic There's that target cost bidding that we talked about in session one. Okay, so I put this in here this is what Facebook talks about with each of those different options with the overarching bid strategy and I'm not going to go through this But it's something that I wanted to put in here for those of you that are interested in kind of talking about this a little bit more So in most cases right those of you that are getting started begin with that auto bidding seven-day click one-day view Just leave it as is don't put any sort of override in there and leave it I wouldn't necessarily try doing target cost bidding has the widest audience because it takes into consideration of largest group of people within a certain attribution window, so if you think about There are more people that are seven-day clickers meaning that they click and go back to something within seven days and That you have a one-day view then our people that are one-day clickers So if you did a seven-day click one-day view That's a bigger audience than people that go and they click on something and it's common that they click on it and they convert within one Day, that's a smaller group of people and as you're starting that can be fairly limiting now some of you may see That's starting with a seven-day click works well or a one-day click works well And if that's the case great, but in most cases I would say start with that auto and not change it But depending on the length of your conversion window as I said that shorter attribution Maybe better so the shorter time window you select the better short-term wins You may see but it'll generally speaker be harder to scale So for example one-day click bid might do great for a period of time But in my experience it's not going to scale as well as a seven-day click one-day view Unless a large percentage of those purchasers buy within one day So that's kind of why you'd want to frame it up like that. So that's what I mean when we talk about the seasoned pixel Strategy you can also use is a strategy of stopping and restarting particular ad sets So this is a suggestion from a member of the power hitters club saying I increase budget daily by 10 to 15 percent and then when the cost per lead starts increase stop scaling and leave it as it is and Then duplicate the ad set and start at a lower budget Then when the performance starts to drop below my goal I'll turn them both off and duplicate one of them and start it again at 70 a day So this is an interesting thing that you can do if you've had the ad sets that let's say do well And then they stop doing well you can stop it duplicate it and restart it as well and that's a Another strategy that people use when scaling and is something that can be really helpful with the aid of Automated rules which we'll talk about Scaling accounts that I see that are successful also do geo expansion so meaning they are targeting in other countries than their own Meaning I've seen this happen in almost every example where they're scaling big time and they're not just in one country So you're utilizing look-alikes, but you're creating them specifically for those countries So you're using the same foundations, but you're you're splitting out countries by look-alike and not grouping them together and so you know the wider those audiences are the better off you'll be and You know there are cost per acquisition and cost per leads in the UK, Australia, Germany, Japan I mean there's things I haven't seen in years That are that are really inexpensive because there's not as much competition in there in those areas And in other countries in the US for sure.
